option: Recognize the 'default AI/template look': centered hero, gradient blob background, Inter font, rounded card grid of 3, generic stock illustration
best_for: Auditing an existing page or a first draft for genericness — this specific combination of choices is the most statistically common output of both page builders and unguided AI generation
avoid_when: Never treat this as a checklist to avoid mechanically — the goal is recognizing the pattern, not superstitiously banning gradients/rounded corners/Inter font, which are all fine in the right combination
tradeoffs: This combination is common precisely because each individual choice is reasonable — the genericness comes from all of them appearing together with no deviation, not from any single choice being wrong

option: Break the vertical-stack-of-centered-sections pattern at least once
best_for: Instead of every section being full-width-centered-column stacked top to bottom, introduce at least one section with asymmetric columns, an off-center focal point, or overlapping elements
avoid_when: A form-heavy or highly transactional flow (checkout, settings) where predictable, centered, linear layout genuinely serves usability better than novelty
tradeoffs: This is `UX034` restated as the flagship technique — it's the single highest-leverage layout-level distinctiveness move because centered-stack is specifically what templates and page builders default to

option: Let content determine section height, don't force uniform viewport-height sections
best_for: Sections are as tall as their content actually needs, producing natural rhythm variation, rather than every section forced to 100vh or a uniform fixed height
avoid_when: A presentation-style single-scroll experience specifically designed around one-section-per-viewport (e.g. a slide-deck-style site) where uniform height is the intended effect
tradeoffs: Forcing uniform section heights is a common page-builder default that produces mechanical, evenly-spaced pacing — natural content-driven height variation is what real editorial layouts do

option: Use an asymmetric or broken grid deliberately for at least one content section
best_for: A gallery, feature showcase, or case-study section uses uneven column spans / intentional overlap rather than a perfectly uniform 3-equal-columns grid
avoid_when: Data tables and information-dense UI where a predictable, uniform grid is a usability requirement, not a stylistic constraint
tradeoffs: A perfectly uniform grid (equal columns, equal gutters, equal card heights) is the fastest visual tell of a component-library default — one deliberately broken grid section signals a real layout decision was made somewhere on the page

option: Use scale contrast aggressively — one huge element against several small ones, not everything sized moderately
best_for: At least one hero moment (a headline, a number, an image) is dramatically larger relative to surrounding content than a 'balanced' composition would suggest
avoid_when: UI/product surfaces (not marketing pages) where predictable, moderate sizing serves task-completion better than dramatic visual hierarchy
tradeoffs: Moderate, evenly-sized elements throughout a composition is the safe default that reads as unconfident — real scale contrast (one big thing, several small things) creates a clear focal point and visual energy that moderate sizing can't

option: Let elements bleed off the edge of the viewport/container intentionally
best_for: An image, a shape, or a text element deliberately extends past its container's edge rather than every element staying safely contained within padding
avoid_when: Content that needs to remain fully visible/scannable in every viewport size — a bleeding element needs to be genuinely decorative, not load-bearing content
tradeoffs: Everything neatly contained within a max-width container with consistent padding is the default, safe choice — one element breaking that containment creates visual interest and signals intentional composition rather than a rigid template being filled in

option: Develop one signature transition/easing curve used consistently, not default library easing everywhere
best_for: Page transitions, modal opens, and key state changes share a custom-tuned easing curve (not the library default ease-in-out) that becomes recognizable as part of the brand's feel
avoid_when: A utility-focused internal tool where motion consistency matters less than raw task speed — default, fast, unobtrusive transitions may be the right call there instead
tradeoffs: A custom easing curve (e.g. a slight overshoot, a specific duration curve) applied consistently is a genuinely underused distinctiveness lever — most products never customize motion timing at all, using whatever a UI library or CSS transition default provides

option: Use motion to reveal spatial relationships (where did this come from, where is it going), not just fade-in everything
best_for: Modals, dropdowns, and expanding elements animate from their actual trigger point/origin rather than a generic fade-and-scale-from-center applied universally
avoid_when: Simple content updates (a list re-sorting, a counter incrementing) where a spatial-origin animation would be more distracting than a simple fade
tradeoffs: Generic fade-in-from-nowhere is easy to implement everywhere but teaches the user nothing about the interface's structure — origin-aware motion (a menu growing from the button that opened it) reinforces the actual spatial model of the UI

option: Let display type be a primary layout/composition element, not just text placed in a text box
best_for: A large headline's specific line breaks, size, and position are treated as compositional choices (like an image would be), not left to responsive text-wrapping defaults alone
avoid_when: Body copy and any UI text where predictable, accessible text flow matters more than compositional intent — this technique is for hero/display moments specifically
tradeoffs: Treating a big headline as 'just text' that autowraps wherever the container happens to break it is a missed opportunity — editorial design treats display type as a shape/composition element with deliberate line breaks and placement

option: Use color-blocking to define zones/sections instead of only borders/whitespace
best_for: Distinct content sections are differentiated by a genuine background color change (not just a subtle gray-on-white shift) creating clear color-block zones down the page
avoid_when: A minimal/restrained brand where color-blocking would conflict with an intentionally quiet, whitespace-driven aesthetic — that's a valid alternative distinctiveness direction, not a gap
tradeoffs: Most default templates separate sections with whitespace and maybe a 1px border — deliberate color-blocking (genuinely different, considered background colors per section) is a stronger, more memorable structural signal

option: A custom cursor state or cursor-following element on key interactive/hero surfaces
best_for: A portfolio, agency, or brand-forward hero section wanting a memorable, slightly playful signature interaction
avoid_when: Any UI where custom cursor behavior could interfere with actual usability/accessibility (this is a hero/brand-moment technique, not a whole-product pattern) — never on task-critical interactive elements
tradeoffs: A custom cursor is a small, cheap, highly noticeable signature technique specifically because almost no product bothers to customize the cursor at all — high memorability per unit of implementation effort, used sparingly

option: Custom, on-brand form field/input styling instead of default browser or library chrome
best_for: Text inputs, checkboxes, radio buttons, and toggles use a considered, custom visual treatment (matching the brand's shape language) rather than the default OS/library appearance left unstyled
avoid_when: Extremely high-density admin/data-entry UI where speed and absolute familiarity of standard controls outweighs brand expression
tradeoffs: Unstyled default form controls are one of the most common places a brand-forward site suddenly reverts to generic browser chrome, breaking an otherwise-considered visual system at exactly the point users interact most directly

option: Commission or create a distinctive illustration style rather than using a generic icon-pack illustration set
best_for: Brand-forward products with budget/time for custom illustration — a consistent, original illustration style is one of the strongest possible distinctiveness signals available
avoid_when: Early-stage/budget-constrained projects where custom illustration isn't feasible — in that case, at minimum apply a consistent treatment (UX040) to whatever stock/library illustrations are used instead
tradeoffs: Generic illustration packs (the same handful of flat-design people-and-shapes illustrations) appear across thousands of unrelated products — recognizable instantly as 'stock' — custom illustration is expensive but has essentially no substitute for genuine visual distinctiveness

option: Vary text alignment deliberately (not everything center- or left-aligned uniformly)
best_for: Specific moments (a pull quote, a large stat, a section intro) use a different alignment than the surrounding default, creating visual variation and emphasis
avoid_when: Body copy and functional UI text, which should stay consistently left-aligned (or right-aligned for RTL) for readability — this technique is for display/emphasis moments only, never paragraph text
tradeoffs: Uniform alignment throughout (usually everything centered, common in template-built sites, or everything left-aligned, common in component-library defaults) is safe but monotonous — deliberate alignment shifts at specific emphasis points break that monotony without hurting body-text readability

option: Treat empty space as an active compositional choice, not just leftover padding between components
best_for: Large expanses of intentional negative space are used to create tension/focus around a small number of elements, rather than filling available space with content 'to use it'
avoid_when: Information-dense dashboards/admin tools where screen real estate is genuinely scarce and negative space is a luxury the use case can't afford
tradeoffs: The instinct to fill available space with more content/cards/features is a common way marketing pages end up feeling cluttered and undifferentiated — deliberately restrained, generous negative space around fewer, more confident elements is a recognizable premium/editorial signal

option: Pick 2-3 distinctiveness techniques from this list and apply them systemically, don't scatter one of everything
best_for: A coherent design direction selects a small number of these techniques (e.g. asymmetric layout + scale contrast + one signature motion curve) and applies them consistently across the whole product
avoid_when: Applying one instance each of many different techniques (a gradient here, a broken grid there, a custom cursor somewhere else) with no unifying logic — that reads as scattered rather than intentional
tradeoffs: This is `UX049` applied at the technique-selection level — genuine distinctiveness comes from a coherent, repeated system of a few deliberate choices, not from sampling every technique in this file once each

option: Screenshot the page next to 3 direct competitors — if it's visually indistinguishable in a lineup, revisit at least one major decision
best_for: A structured, concrete version of `UX050`'s review question — actually place screenshots side by side rather than relying on memory/impression of how distinctive the page feels
avoid_when: Never skip this for a genuinely brand-critical page (homepage, primary landing page) — for a minor internal/utility page, the bar for distinctiveness is appropriately lower
tradeoffs: This is the single most reliable way to catch genericness, because it's very difficult to accurately judge a design's distinctiveness from inside the process of building it incrementally — an external side-by-side comparison makes the gap visible immediately
